paper.title: New Trends in Treating Cushing's Disease

entities.name: LOGICS trial
synonyms: A study to assess the safety and efficacy of levoketoconazole in the treatment of endogenous Cushing's syndrome

entities.name: LINC3 trial
synonyms: Safety and efficacy of LCI699 for the treatment of patients with Cushing's disease

entities.name: LINC4 trial
synonyms: Efficacy and safety evaluation of osilodrostat in Cushing's disease

entities.name: GRACE trial
synonyms: A study of the efficacy and safety of relacorilant in patients with endogenous Cushing syndrome

entities.name: GRADIENT trial
synonyms: Efficacy and safety of relacorilant in patients with cortisol-secreting adrenal adenomas

evidence_entities.text: Cushing's disease (CD) is caused by an adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH)-secreting pituitary adenoma, or rarely carcinoma, and is considered a highly morbid endocrine disorder with few medical options.

Although transsphenoidal pituitary surgery (TSS) is the mainstay of treatment for this disease, up to one-third of the patients eventually relapse and are required to be treated with a variety of drugs, as none can achieve optimal disease control.
Pasireotide is a novel somatostatin analogue targeting four out of five SSTR subtypes, with the highest affinity for SSTR5.

The first randomized phase III clinical trial involving 162 patients with CD treated with subcutaneous pasireotide showed urine-free cortisol (UFC) normalization at month 6 in 15–26% of the patients without uptitration, and, simultaneously, a significant clinical improvement was noted in most patients.
Despite the benefits of pasireotide, about two-thirds of the patients developed hyperglycaemia-related adverse events, requiring additional antidiabetic treatment.
Starting at a dose of 300 mg/day and titrated up to 1,200 mg/day, levoketoconazole normalized mean urinary free cortisol (mUFC) levels in more than 50% of the patients.
At the end of the core study (at week 48), osilodrostat resulted in a complete response in 66% and a partial response (≥50% reduction of UFC) in 9% of the enrolled patients, respectively.

A phase II study over 20 weeks showed that, after dose stabilization, 42% and 15% of the patients coming from the low-dose group (200 mg/day) demonstrated a blood pressure reduction of ≥5 mmHg and a glycaemic reduction, respectively, whereas in the high-dose group (400 mg/day), an improvement in hypertension and glucose metabolism was achieved in 64% and 50%, respectively.

notes: This paper is a comprehensive review of new therapeutic approaches for Cushing's disease, focusing on novel drugs targeting different mechanisms including pituitary-directed agents, steroidogenesis inhibitors, and immunotherapy approaches.
The paper includes detailed clinical trial data for several drugs including pasireotide, levoketoconazole, osilodrostat, and relacorilant, with specific efficacy and safety profiles.
Multiple molecular targets are discussed including somatostatin receptors, USP8, EGFR, BRAF, and immune checkpoint proteins, representing a shift toward personalized medicine approaches.
